era_context: The Classical Greek world in the mid-4th century BCE, with Thebes at the height of its power following Leuctra (371 BCE). Sparta's hegemony had been broken, but Theban dominance was not yet secure. Philip II of Macedon was consolidating his kingdom in the north. The Persian Achaemenid Empire remained vast but internally unstable. In China, the Warring States period was in full swing.

events.title: Theban hegemony at its height; Epaminondas dominates Greek politics
events.description: In 369 BCE, Thebes under Epaminondas was at the height of its power and influence. Epaminondas had consolidated Theban control over Boeotia and was the dominant figure in Greek politics. The Theban hegemony, established after Leuctra in 371 BCE, was secure and at its peak. Thebes was the most powerful Greek city-state, though the rise of Philip II of Macedon in the north was beginning to pose a challenge to Greek independence.
events.certainty_note: Xenophon's Hellenica provides near-contemporary evidence. Later sources provide narrative detail but are written centuries later. The general trajectory of Theban power is secure; specific events in 369 BCE are less precisely dated.
cross_references: -362-001 (Battle of Mantinea, end of Theban hegemony)

events.title: Artaxerxes II's reign continues; Persian Empire faces internal challenges
events.description: Artaxerxes II (ruled 404–358 BCE) was in the middle of his long reign. In 369 BCE, the Persian Empire remained the largest and most powerful state in the Near East, but it faced chronic internal instability. Egypt remained in revolt, and various satraps and dynastic rivals posed challenges to central authority. The reign was characterized by the rise of powerful eunuchs and court factions.
events.certainty_note: The general trajectory of Artaxerxes II's reign is well-known from Greek sources, but specific events in 369 BCE are not well-documented. Archaeological evidence confirms the empire's continued operation but does not provide year-specific information.

events.title: Philip II of Macedon consolidates power; early military campaigns
events.description: Philip II was in the early-to-middle years of his reign, consolidating Macedonian power and beginning to expand northward and eastward. By 369 BCE, he had secured his throne against internal rivals and was establishing Macedon as a major regional power. His military reforms were being implemented, and he was beginning to pose a threat to the Greek city-states to the south.
events.certainty_note: The chronology of Philip's early reign is uncertain. Sources disagree on the dates of specific campaigns. The general trajectory is clear, but year-specific events are difficult to pin down.

events.title: Warring States period: military innovation and state consolidation in China
events.description: During the Warring States period, Chinese states continued their competition for supremacy. Around 369 BCE, Qin was beginning to emerge as a major power in the west, while Chu dominated the south. The state of Zhao in the north was also expanding. This period saw continuous military innovation and the development of Legalist philosophy, which emphasized strict laws and military discipline.
key_figures: Various rulers of Qin, Chu, Zhao, and other states

sources.name: Shiji (Records of the Grand Historian) by Sima Qian
Archaeological evidence from Warring States sites

events.certainty_note: Chinese chronology for the Warring States period is uncertain by several years. The Shiji was compiled centuries later. Archaeological evidence provides material confirmation but not precise year-by-year dating.

disconfirming_evidence: No major disconfirming evidence identified for events listed in 369 BCE. However, the chronology of Philip II's early reign is debated, and the Warring States chronology in China is notoriously uncertain. The general trajectories described are secure, but year-specific dating should be treated as approximate.
historiographic_note: The year 369 BCE is moderately well-documented for the Greek world, primarily through later compilations and Xenophon's near-contemporary Hellenica. For Persia, we rely on Greek accounts and archaeological evidence. Chinese sources are entirely post-hoc compilations with uncertain chronology. The historical record is heavily weighted toward the Mediterranean and Near East. Sub-Saharan Africa, the Americas, and much of Central Asia have no surviving written records for this year.

graph_edges.note: Theban hegemony under Epaminondas in 369 BCE led to the Battle of Mantinea (362 BCE), where Epaminondas died and Theban dominance ended.
Philip II's consolidation of Macedonian power in the 360s BCE led to his dominance of Greece by 338 BCE.
